content.text: PCI Scope Analysis for GenAI Data Flows

Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) v4.0 places strict controls on every system that stores, processes, or transmits cardholder data (CHD) and sensitive authentication data (SAD). As GenAI workloads enter the payment technology stack — answering customer queries, detecting fraud, or generating financial summaries — teams must precisely determine which AI components fall within the cardholder data environment (CDE) and which can remain outside it.

Getting scope wrong in either direction is costly: under-scoping triggers compliance gaps and breach liability; over-scoping inflates audit effort, slows delivery, and drives up operational cost. This sub-subtopic walks through the PCI DSS v4.0 scoping framework applied to real GenAI architecture patterns, together with code-level controls to isolate and de-scope CHD from AI pipelines wherever possible.
PCI DSS v4.0 defines three categories of system components:

content: Systems that store, process, or transmit CHD or SAD, and systems that are connected to them via same-network segments or data flows carrying CHD.
Systems that connect to the CDE — they do not themselves store CHD, but must meet hardening and monitoring controls because they can affect CDE security.

Systems with no pathway (network or data) to CHD or the CDE. Must be provably isolated — network segmentation, tokenisation, or strict data handling that prevents CHD from ever reaching these components.

content.text: be stored, logged, or passed to any AI model — including in prompt text, fine-tuning datasets, or RAG corpora. This is an absolute prohibition, not a compensating-control situation.
GenAI Architecture Patterns and Scope Impact

attrs.alt: PCI Scope Analysis for GenAI Data Flows

content.text: The diagram above maps common GenAI components to their PCI scope category. The core principle: any component that receives or emits raw CHD/SAD is CDE; components that receive only tokenised or masked representations must still meet security-control requirements.
Pattern 1: Customer-Facing Chat with Payment Context

A support chatbot that can answer questions like "what is the last transaction on my Visa card ending 4321?" almost certainly sits in the CDE. The response buffer contains CHD (the last-four digits, at minimum), and the prompt pipeline may receive full account numbers if not pre-filtered.

content: CDE. Apply Req 3 (protect stored CHD), Req 4 (encrypt in transit), Req 7 (access control), Req 10 (logging).
Pre-process user input through a PAN tokeniser before the prompt reaches the LLM. If the model only ever sees tokens (e.g.
), the LLM compute layer itself can potentially exit the CDE, reducing audit scope significantly.

content.text: Pattern 2: Fraud Detection Model Using Transaction History

An ML/LLM model trained on historical transactions for fraud detection may have been fine-tuned on datasets containing real PANs. Even if the trained model weights do not directly output CHD, the training pipeline and the stored weights inherit CDE status if they were derived from CHD.

content: Training pipeline = CDE. Model weights = CDE artefact (until provably stripped of CHD influence via techniques like differential privacy or full re-training on synthetic data).

Use synthetic data generation (GANs, statistical sampling) for training. Keep real CHD only in the feature engineering step, producing anonymised feature vectors that become the actual training input.

content.text: Pattern 3: RAG Pipeline over Policy Documents

A RAG system that retrieves from an internal knowledge base of policy PDFs poses minimal PCI risk — provided those PDFs contain no CHD/SAD. The embedding store and retrieval path are out of scope if the source documents are free of cardholder data.

However, if policy documents are generated from transaction systems and inadvertently embed PAN data (e.g. sample transaction in a policy PDF), the entire RAG store becomes CDE. A document sanitisation pipeline is required before ingestion.
The safest GenAI scope-reduction technique is to intercept CHD before it enters the AI pipeline and replace it with a non-CHD token that the AI can reason about without triggering PCI obligations.

import re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class PANDetectorResult(Enum):
    CLEAN    = "clean"
    PAN_FOUND = "pan_found"
    SAD_FOUND = "sad_found"

# Regex catches 13–19 digit sequences (Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover)
_PAN_RE = re.compile(r'\b(?:\d[ -]?){12,18}\d\b')
# CVV: 3-4 digits near keywords
_CVV_RE = re.compile(r'\b(cvv|cvc|cvc2|cvv2|cav|security code)[:\s]*\d{3,4}\b', re.IGNORECASE)
# Full mag-stripe sentinel
_TRACK_RE = re.compile(r'%B\d{13,19}\^', re.IGNORECASE)

def _luhn_check(number: str) -> bool:
    digits = [int(d) for d in number if d.isdigit()]
    total = 0
    for i, d in enumerate(reversed(digits)):
        if i % 2 == 1:
            d *= 2
            if d > 9:
                d -= 9
        total += d
    return total % 10 == 0

@dataclass
class PANScanResult:
    result: PANDetectorResult
    count: int
    masked_text: str   # safe to log; PANs replaced with XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-last4

def scan_and_mask(text: str) -> PANScanResult:
    """
    Scan text for PAN/SAD patterns.
    Returns a masked version safe to pass to the LLM.
    Raises ValueError if SAD (magnetic stripe / CVV) is detected —
    those must never be forwarded even in masked form.
    """
    if _TRACK_RE.search(text):
        raise ValueError("Magnetic-stripe track data detected — request blocked")
    if _CVV_RE.search(text):
        raise ValueError("CVV/CVC data detected — request blocked")

    found = 0
    masked = text

    for m in reversed(list(_PAN_RE.finditer(text))):
        digits = re.sub(r'[ -]', '', m.group())
        if len(digits) < 13 or not _luhn_check(digits):
            continue
        found += 1
        last4 = digits[-4:]
        replacement = f"XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-{last4}"
        masked = masked[:m.start()] + replacement + masked[m.end():]

    status = PANDetectorResult.PAN_FOUND if found else PANDetectorResult.CLEAN
    return PANScanResult(result=status, count=found, masked_text=masked)

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
import hashlib

@dataclass
class PromptScanRecord:
    request_id: str
    timestamp: str
    pan_count: int
    sad_blocked: bool
    forwarded: bool      # True = prompt reached LLM; False = blocked

class PANFilterMiddleware:
    """
    Drop-in middleware for any LLM client.
    Scans every prompt, masks PANs, blocks SAD.
    Emits an audit record for PCI Req 10 compliance.
    """
    def __init__(self, llm_client, audit_sink):
        self._llm = llm_client
        self._sink = audit_sink

    def chat(self, messages: list[dict], **kwargs):
        clean_messages = []
        aggregate_pan = 0
        blocked = False

        for msg in messages:
            raw = msg.get("content", "")
            try:
                result = scan_and_mask(raw)
                aggregate_pan += result.count
                clean_messages.append({**msg, "content": result.masked_text})
            except ValueError as exc:
                # SAD detected — block entire request
                blocked = True
                self._audit(aggregate_pan, sad_blocked=True, forwarded=False)
                raise PermissionError(f"Request blocked by PCI filter: {exc}") from exc

        self._audit(aggregate_pan, sad_blocked=False, forwarded=True)
        return self._llm.chat(clean_messages, **kwargs)

    def _audit(self, pan_count, sad_blocked, forwarded):
        import uuid
        rec = PromptScanRecord(
            request_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
            timestamp=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
            pan_count=pan_count,
            sad_blocked=sad_blocked,
            forwarded=forwarded,
        )
        self._sink.write(rec)
The middleware pattern keeps scope-reduction logic entirely out of application code. Development teams call
identically to the underlying LLM client — the PAN masking and audit trail are transparent.

A RAG embedding pipeline must sanitise source documents before vectorising them. If a document escapes sanitisation and is embedded with a PAN, every downstream retrieval query that surfaces that chunk carries CDE risk — even if no PAN was in the query itself.

from pathlib import Path
import json

class RAGSanitiser:
    """
    Pre-ingestion sanitiser for RAG corpora.
    Call sanitise() before passing text to the embedding model.
    """
    def __init__(self, quarantine_dir: str = "/tmp/pci_quarantine"):
        self._quarantine = Path(quarantine_dir)
        self._quarantine.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    def sanitise(self, doc_id: str, text: str) -> tuple[str, bool]:
        """
        Returns (sanitised_text, was_modified).
        If SAD is detected the document is quarantined and ValueError raised.
        """
        try:
            result = scan_and_mask(text)
        except ValueError:
            self._quarantine_doc(doc_id, text)
            raise

        if result.count > 0:
            # Document contained PANs — log and return masked version.
            # Optionally reject entirely (stricter posture).
            import logging
            logging.warning(
                "PCI: doc %s contained %d PAN(s); masked before embedding",
                doc_id, result.count
            )
            return result.masked_text, True

        return text, False

    def _quarantine_doc(self, doc_id: str, text: str):
        path = self._quarantine / f"{doc_id}.quarantine"
        path.write_text(json.dumps({
            "doc_id": doc_id,
            "reason": "SAD_DETECTED",
            "content_hash": __import__('hashlib').sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest(),
            # Never write the actual text to disk in cleartext
        }))
PCI DSS v4.0 Requirements Mapped to GenAI Controls
Do not retain SAD after authorisation
Block SAD at prompt middleware; quarantine any corpus document containing SAD
Store only last-four PAN in LLM logs and embedding metadata; use format-preserving encryption for any CHD at rest
TLS 1.2+ for all LLM API calls; verify certificate chains in client code
Protect all system components from vulnerabilities
Pin LLM SDK versions; scan container images; apply prompt injection mitigations
RBAC on LLM endpoints; separate service accounts for CDE vs non-CDE AI services
MFA for any human operator with access to model weights, training data, or vector DB
WORM storage for prompt scan records; retention per Req 10.5 (12 months, 3 months online)
Log every prompt scan result with request_id, timestamp, PAN count (never the PAN itself)
Include LLM prompt injection and data exfiltration scenarios in pen test scope for CDE-connected AI components
Document GenAI-specific risks in annual targeted risk analysis: training data leakage, model inversion, indirect prompt injection

PCI DSS v4.0 Req 11.4.5 requires testing of segmentation controls at least every six months (annually for service providers). GenAI architectures complicate segmentation because cloud-managed LLM APIs bypass traditional network perimeters.
Segmentation Test Checklist for GenAI Components

content: Confirm outbound traffic to LLM provider endpoints is routed through a controlled egress point (NAT gateway, proxy) with DNS logging — no direct internet access from CDE servers.
If the vector DB is populated from CHD and thus CDE-scoped, confirm no direct network path exists from out-of-scope systems to the DB port.
Training clusters with CHD training data must be on isolated VPCs/VLANs with no route to production CDE.
S3 buckets / object stores holding CDE-derived model weights must have bucket policies restricting access to CDE-role IAM entities only.
The API Gateway should be the single choke-point — all traffic passes through its PAN-filtering middleware before reaching any CDE component.

content.text: QSAs (Qualified Security Assessors) expect specific documentation for GenAI components. Prepare the following for each AI component assessed:

content: Show every system that sends data to or receives data from the AI component. Mark where CHD enters, where it is masked/tokenised, and what exits the CDE boundary.

For each out-of-scope AI component, provide a written rationale: "This component receives only tokenised data. No CHD/SAD traverses its network segment. Segmentation is verified by [test date/method]"
List every dataset used to train or fine-tune deployed models. Classify each as CHD, pseudonymised, synthetic, or no-CHD. Provide lineage from source system to training artefact.
Evidence that prompt middleware is operational: log extracts showing request IDs, scan timestamps, PAN-count fields (no actual PANs), and block events.

If using a hosted LLM API (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.), obtain their PCI DSS Attestation of Compliance (AoC) or confirm they are on the Visa/Mastercard service provider list. Understand what data you send and what their shared responsibility model covers.

content.text: Logging raw prompts to CloudWatch / Splunk:

content: If prompts may contain CHD, the logging infrastructure becomes CDE. Mask before logging, or log only the scan-record metadata (request_id, PAN count).
PCI DSS permits storing the last four digits, but storing full PAN + last four in separate fields is still a violation if the full PAN is accessible. Ensure the original PAN is irreversibly masked.
Intermediate model checkpoints saved during fine-tuning inherit CDE status if training data contains CHD. Apply the same access controls as the final weights.
"We send data to OpenAI" does not eliminate your PCI obligations. You remain responsible for what CHD you transmit, your TLS configuration, and the contractual controls in place with the provider.

A customer service transcript may contain "my CVV is 123" in free text. Pattern matching on structured fields is not enough — deploy the regex-based SAD scanner described above on all free-text inputs.

content.text: PCI DSS v4.0 scope analysis for GenAI data flows follows the same foundational rules as traditional CDE scoping, but requires careful attention to new surfaces: prompt pipelines, embedding stores, model weights, and training data lineage.
scope reduction through tokenisation and masking

: intercept CHD at the earliest possible point, replace it with a non-CHD token, and design AI components downstream of that interception point to handle only the token. This approach can push the majority of the GenAI stack out of the CDE, dramatically reducing audit scope and operational burden.

When CHD cannot be avoided — fraud detection models trained on real transactions, customer-facing bots that must reference account details — apply CDE controls rigorously: encryption at rest and in transit, strict RBAC, comprehensive logging (of metadata, never raw CHD), and network segmentation validated every six months.

Maintain documentation artefacts that a QSA can follow: annotated data flow diagrams, scope justification narratives, training data inventories, and evidence of prompt-scan middleware operation. Proactive documentation before an assessment window reduces finding risk and accelerates the audit timeline.
